evidence: Person missing recommended: jobTitle, sameAs, hasCredential
FAQPage schema has 7 Questions but page shows ~13 FAQ pairs

No TL;DR block (expected

or paragraph starting with 'TL;DR:')
No visible 'Last updated' / 'Last reviewed' / 'Next review' stamp on page (schema dateModified alone isn't enough for users or AI)
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
Specific human voice vs committee 4/10 — Mostly corporate editorial voice with occasional injected 'spicy' lines that feel pasted in.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 5/10 — A few pointed opinions surface (lab numbers, annual physicals are theatre) but most claims are hedged.
Unexpected phrasings 6/10 — Some sharp lines ('the desert humbles every calendar', 'Ramadan eats your routine') but surrounded by generic filler.
Specific citations (names, prices, dates) 6/10 — Mentions AED 1,200, 19 March 2026, DHA, MOHAP, but no specific clinics, study authors, or personal data.
Point of view vs both-sides survey 3/10 — Surveys guidelines rather than taking a stance.
Quotability 2/10 — Nothing memorable enough to repeat.
Intro earns attention 3/10 — Opens with TL;DR boilerplate and a generic 'Dubai residents often...' line; the interesting hook sits buried mid-article.

intro_hook 4/10 — The opening sentence is a generic demographic statement with zero tension or specificity. It's functionally identical to 'In today's world, many people...' The TL;DR above it is strong, but the actual prose hook is flat and earns no attention. The body later contains a genuinely compelling line ('By mid-March, I've watched half my clients quietly abandon January's goals') buried in Key Takeaways—that energy needs to open the post.

specificity 6/10 — The post has pockets of good specificity (AED 1,200 panel cost, named nutrients, Ramadan timing) but several sections drift into abstraction. Phrases like 'rapidly shifting demands,' 'current circumstances,' 'long-term health change,' and 'small, regionally aware changes' float without landing on concrete anchors. The vitamin D / fasting glucose / lipid list is strong; the behaviour-change sections are weak. The suggested rewrite adds a named gym chain, a specific NCMS temperature reference, and sharpens causality.
